nameMeanings.name: Corbert
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: English (West Midlands, of Norman origin): nickname from Old French corbet ‘raven’, probably denoting someone with dark hair or a dark complexion.

History: This is the name of a family descended from Hugh Corbet, a Norman baron who settled in Shropshire following the Norman Conquest. One of his descendants, Sir Richard Corbet, was granted land near Shrewsbury in 1223; since the 13th century, this place has been known as Moreton Corbet. The name was taken from Shropshire to Scotland in the 12th century and to northern Ireland in the 17th century, and thence to North America by at least one group of bearers of the name.

variants.name: Corbit
meanings: Scottish: variant of   Corbett  . This surname is now rare in Britain.

variants.name: Corb
meanings: English (Durham): nickname from Middle English corb ‘raven’ (from Old French corb; compare Corbett , Corbin ).

variants.name: Corbet
meanings: History: Walter Corbet, a royal knight in 12th-century Scotland, was the first of that name in that kingdom. His 13th-century descendants were major landholders in the Borders, and in the 14th century they acquired lands in the North of Scotland.

variants.name: Corban
meanings: Irish: shortened Anglicized form of Ó Corbáin ‘descendant of Corbán’ or Ó Coirbín ‘descendant of Coirbín’, both possibly from an occupational name based on corb ‘chariot’. Both names have also been Anglicized as Corbett .
Irish: alternatively, a variant of Currivan, from Ó Coirbheáin, itself a variant of Corban (pronounced with three syllables in Irish).
Romanian: nickname derived from corb ‘crow’, with the augmentative suffix -an.

nameMeanings.name: Robert
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: One of the many French names of Germanic origin that were introduced into Britain by the Normans; it has since remained in continuous use. It is derived from the nearly synonymous elements hrōd ‘fame’ + berht ‘bright, famous’, and had a native Old English predecessor of similar form (Hreodbeorht), which was supplanted by the Norman name. Two dukes of Normandy in the 11th century bore the name: the father of William the Conqueror (sometimes identified with the legendary Robert the Devil), and his eldest son. It was borne also by three kings of Scotland, notably Robert the Bruce ( 1274–1329 ), who freed Scotland from English domination. The altered short form Bob is very common, but Hob and Dob, which were common in the Middle Ages and gave rise to surnames, are extinct. See also Rupert .

en.lifeSummary: Robert Corbert was born in 1220, in Moreton by Oswestry, Shropshire, England, United Kingdom. He had at least 1 son. He died in 1301, in Shropshire, England, United Kingdom, at the age of 81.
